justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups (B2C recipients, B2B/enterprise, customer support, operations/dispatch, sales/account management, product/marketing, legal/compliance) each with specific impact descriptions. 4 escalations cover missing information that cannot be assumed (tier scope, display-vs-data-model, motivation, API contracts). Contractual SLA risk is explicitly and repeatedly identified (BR-2, rejection_reasons). confidence.level is 45, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning is mentioned in both the financial impact and the product/marketing stakeholder section.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 7 distinct groups, far exceeding the minimum 3 required
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — correctly identifies the proposal is underspecified and flags missing contract information
4 formal escalations with structured options and recommendations, covering exactly the ambiguities the rubric expects
Contractual SLA risk is a central theme, identified in business rules, risk section, escalations, and rejection_reasons
Competitive positioning explicitly called out — Amazon, FedEx, UPS trending toward more precision
Goes beyond rubric requirements with rejection_reasons that tie violations back to specific business rules
Operational impact is balanced — acknowledges the benefit (reduced pressure) while identifying the cost (loss of optimization data)
Edge cases are excellent — cross-timezone ambiguity, same-day delivery, mid-transit tier upgrade, B2B API breaking changes
Output is extremely verbose for what could be a more focused business impact assessment — though thoroughness is not penalized by the rubric
